meta.disclaimer: Generated from public market data and news for research and education. Not financial advice; data may be delayed, incomplete, or wrong.

items.title: EEVEE: Towards Test-time Prompt Learning in the Real World for Self-Improving Agents
items.abstract: EEVEE introduces a novel test-time prompt learning framework designed for real-world, heterogeneous task streams, overcoming limitations of single-dataset methods. Its core method involves a router that clusters incoming inputs and assigns them to appropriate prompt configurations, optimized through a router-prompt co-evolution strategy. This approach significantly improves the robustness of LLM agents when handling diverse, interleaved data while preserving performance on individual tasks.
items.figure_caption: Incremental multi-benchmark retention improvement as tasks are added in the order GPQA Diamond, Formula, TheoremQA, and HumanEval. Each bar stacks per-benchmark improvements for all tasks seen so far: solid upward blocks are positive gains, and hatched downward blocks are negative retention losses. The number above or below each bar is its final summed improvement after all blocks are added.

items.title: Mind the Gap: Can Frontier LLMs Pass a Standardized Office Proficiency Exam?
items.abstract: This paper introduces a rigorous benchmark, based on China's National Computer Rank Examination (NCRE), to evaluate frontier Large Language Models' (LLMs) ability to perform complex, multi-application Office automation tasks requiring long-horizon planning. The evaluation uses 200 practical tasks scored against 7,118 criteria. The core contribution is demonstrating the significant limitations of current LLMs in professional software proficiency, with even strong agentic systems showing limited success in passing this standardized office exam.
items.figure_caption: End-to-end illustration of a Word task in OfficeEval . The original document ( left ) is transformed according to the task instructions ( center ) into a styled brochure with header image, heading styles, and mail-merge labels ( right ). Only page 1 of the 2-page document is shown; several steps (e.g., 3-column layout, watermark) apply to page 2. The task is scored by 30 deterministic criteria across 6 skill categories. Instructions are translated from the original Chinese; additional examples across Word, Excel, and PowerPoint appear in the Appendix.

items.title: Null-Space Constrained Low-Rank Adaptation for Response-Specified Large Language Model Unlearning
items.abstract: This paper introduces Null-Space Constrained Response-Specified Unlearning (NSRU), a low-rank adaptation method for LLM unlearning. NSRU constrains the update parameters to the null space of estimated "retain subspaces" derived from benign data, ensuring adaptation is localized. This method jointly optimizes suppressing undesired responses, learning a safe target response, and preserving benign capabilities.
items.figure_caption: Motivation and core intuition of NSRU. (a) Suppression-only unlearning penalizes the undesired response y − y^{-} but leaves the safe replacement behavior unspecified and can induce under-constrained updates that perturb retained behavior. (b) NSRU specifies a safe target response y + y^{+} , explicitly suppresses y − y^{-} , and uses projected LoRA updates that act through retain-orthogonal components, redirecting forget queries while reducing retain-side interference.

items.title: ReasonAlloc: Hierarchical Decoding-Time KV Cache Budget Allocation for Reasoning Models
items.abstract: ReasonAlloc addresses KV cache bottlenecks in LLM reasoning by introducing a hierarchical, training-free budget allocation framework. It combines an offline layer-wise preallocation strategy, capturing the "Reasoning Wave" demand pattern, with an online head-wise reallocation strategy that prioritizes information-rich heads during decoding. This dynamic approach significantly mitigates inference latency caused by long CoT trajectories more effectively than uniform or static allocation methods.
items.figure_caption: An overview of the proposed ReasonAlloc framework. Left (I): Layer-wise allocation strategy based on offline architecture calibration, demonstrating the non-linear “Reasoning Wave” KV demand across layers. Right (II): Head-wise allocation strategy that dynamically routes KV budgets to distinct attention heads based on real-time importance and redundancy scoring during decoding.

items.title: Role-Agent: Bootstrapping LLM Agents via Dual-Role Evolution
items.abstract: The Role-Agent framework bootstraps LLM agent learning by having a single LLM concurrently act as both the agent and the environment. It uses a dual-component system: World-In-Agent (WIA) generates a process reward based on state prediction accuracy, while Agent-In-World (AIW) uses failure analysis to reshape the training data for targeted improvement. This self-contained co-evolution addresses limitations of static environments and inefficient feedback, leading to enhanced generalization.
items.figure_caption: (a): Static environments provide sparse and non-specific feedback that limits the agent’s exploration; (b): Synthetic environments incur high labor and runtime costs; (c): The proposed Role-Agent enables one model to switch roles between agent and environment to achieve bootstrapped co-evolution.

items.title: T1-Bench: Benchmarking Multi-Scenario Agents in Real-World Domains
items.abstract: T1-Bench is introduced as a high-fidelity benchmark designed to evaluate LLM-based agents in complex, realistic, multi-domain customer-facing scenarios. Its core contribution is providing a standardized framework that captures sustained reasoning and coordination across interleaved, multi-turn interactions, significantly increasing compositional complexity and evaluative rigor compared to existing benchmarks.
items.figure_caption: Overview of T1-Bench , a framework for persistent multi-session conversational agents. User policies, including persona, user information, and goals, guide interactions between the user and the assistant. The assistant performs domain classification to retrieve domain-specific tools and policies (e.g., flight, hotel, and restaurant services) and executes tool-augmented reasoning via API calls. A shared memory module stores conversation history and cached results, enabling persistent context, tool reuse, and continuity across temporally separated sessions.

items.title: What Fits (Into Few Tokens) Doesn't Overfit: Compression and Generalization in ML Research Agents
items.abstract: This paper investigates the hypothesis that successful machine learning strategies are highly compressible, even when adaptively reused on held-out benchmarks. The authors test this using LLM-driven research agents under two compression bottlenecks: limiting the agent's prompt (output compression) or restricting feedback to one bit (input compression). They find that these compression methods have little effect on the final performance achieved across diverse ML tasks, suggesting that the successful search strategies are inherently compact.
items.figure_caption: Compressibility of an autonomous agent’s language model pre-training strategy. An explorer agent develops a 12-layer, 768-dimensional GPT with ∼ 30 {\( \sim \)}30 non-default choices; we compress its strategy into progressively shorter prompts (64 down to 4 tokens) and hand each to 5 independent reproducers that implement the strategy without the explorer’s code or validation set. Left : compressed prompts. Tokens encode architecture ( 12L d768 ), optimizer ( Muon .1 ), activation ( ReLU 2 ), batch size ( b2M ), and normalization ( QKnorm ); color indicates survival depth (dark blue = survives tightest budgets, red = dropped first). Right : reproducer holdout loss (BPB); dot = mean, gray band = min–max, dashed line = explorer before compression. Performance holds to 16 tokens; a cliff at 8 tokens coincides with loss of batch size, MLP ratio, and QK-norm.

items.title: Workflow-GYM: Towards Long-Horizon Evaluation of Computer-use Agentic tasks in Real-World Professional Fields
items.abstract: Workflow-GYM is introduced as a novel benchmark to address the lack of evaluation for AI agents performing long-horizon, high-value professional workflows using graphical user interfaces (GUIs). The core method involves creating tasks centered on specialized, domain-specific professional software environments. The contribution is demonstrating that current state-of-the-art agents struggle significantly, achieving only about 30% success rates on these complex, real-world professional tasks.
items.figure_caption: Examples of Workflow-GYM tasks from professional domains. Each task requires interacting with specialized software through graphical user interfaces to accomplish a real-world objective.

items.title: Flow-DPPO: Divergence Proximal Policy Optimization for Flow Matching Models
items.abstract: Flow-DPPO addresses limitations in applying standard PPO to flow matching models by replacing noisy ratio clipping with a direct divergence constraint. Leveraging the Gaussian nature of the per-step policy, it enables exact and efficient computation of the KL divergence between old and new policies. This method provides a more structurally sound trust region enforcement, leading to improved quality and alignment in generative models.
items.figure_caption: Qualitative comparison on FLUX.1-dev (Black Forest Labs, 2024 ) with GenEval2 (Kamath et al. , 2025 ) prompts. Flow-DPPO achieves competitive compositional accuracy with notably less image quality degradation compared to Flow-GRPO (Liu et al. , 2025 ) , Flow-CPS (Wang and Yu, 2025 ) , and GRPO-Guard (Wang et al. , 2025 ) , reflecting their superior KL-proximal efficiency.

items.title: Does Reasoning Preserve Alignment? On the Trustworthiness of Large Reasoning Models
items.abstract: This paper investigates whether converting instruction-tuned Large Language Models (LLMs) into reasoning models via post-training preserves their original alignment behaviors (safety, bias avoidance, etc.). The core method involves a systematic trustworthiness audit comparing reasoning models (trained via SFT, RL, or distillation) against their instruction-tuned baselines across six dimensions. The key contribution is demonstrating that this conversion often leads to significant alignment regressions, despite improved reasoning accuracy.
items.figure_caption: Reasoning model development pathways. We study three common pathways for converting instruction-tuned models into reasoning models: (1) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on reasoning traces, (2) RL-based post-training, including GRPO-style variants, using reasoning-oriented rewards, and (3) distillation from stronger reasoning teachers. Each reasoning model is evaluated against a matched instruction-tuned baseline to measure how the conversion affects reasoning utility and trustworthiness.

items.title: It Takes One to Bias Them All: Breaking Bad with One-Shot GRPO
items.abstract: This paper demonstrates that a single biased example, introduced via one-shot Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), is sufficient to induce systematic and generalizing bias in large language models (LLMs). The core contribution is revealing a critical vulnerability where post-training alignment guardrails can be easily overridden by minimal targeted adversarial training. Model susceptibility is shown to correlate with its initial propensity for biased outputs.

items.title: Pushing the Limits of LLM Tool Calling via Experiential Knowledge Integration and Activation
items.abstract: This paper investigates how to improve LLM tool-calling by integrating and activating experiential knowledge. The core method involves acquiring instance-level knowledge, which proves highly effective, and employing parallel sampling (expanding reasoning width) during inference to better activate this knowledge. The contribution lies in demonstrating that simple instance knowledge and parallel reasoning are superior strategies for enhancing multi-step tool-use performance.
items.figure_caption: The augmentation results of different experiential knowledge. “All” indicates incorporating all the experiential knowledge.

items.title: The Shibboleth Effect: Auditing the Cross-Lingual Distributional Skew of Large Language Models
items.abstract: This paper introduces the "Shibboleth Effect," examining how frontier LLMs exhibit cross-lingual distributional skew under adversarial conditions. Using a simulated geopolitical wargame played in English versus Turkish, the authors found that models display heterogeneous behavioral changes, such as Llama-4 significantly increasing coercive rhetoric when prompted in Turkish. The core contribution is demonstrating that language choice directly and differentially biases LLM strategic behavior.

items.title: A History-Aware Visually Grounded Critic for Computer Use Agents
items.abstract: This paper introduces **HiViG**, a history-aware, visually grounded critic framework for Computer Use Agents (CUAs). HiViG addresses limitations in existing critics by training a multimodal model on real GUI trajectories to summarize past interactions and verify proposed actions against the current screen visuals. This provides agents with both long-term context and precise visual grounding to detect flawed execution steps during operation.
items.figure_caption: Comparison of test-time interventions for Computer Use Agents (CUAs). Left : Lacking historical awareness and proactive error-recovery, standard policies easily become trapped in short-sighted decision loops. Right : Existing approaches are limited. Scalar feedback (top) traps policies in low-reward trajectory regions when all candidate actions are suboptimal. Previous critics (middle) rely heavily on textual intent, missing spatial and reasoning errors, and fail to provide historical awareness. In contrast, our critic (bottom) verifies raw execution coordinates, predicts immediate visual state outcomes grounded in its learned state transition knowledge, and provides visually grounded error analysis that intercepts errors before execution. Furthermore, it equips agents with history state tracking, condensing past interactions to guide them toward the final task objective.

items.title: ABC-Bench: An Agentic Bio-Capabilities Benchmark for Biosecurity
items.abstract: The paper introduces **ABC-Bench**, a novel benchmark designed to systematically evaluate the agentic biosecurity-relevant capabilities of Large Language Model (LLM) agents. This benchmark assesses both beneficial and dual-use biology tasks, such as robotic coding and DNA design, requiring integrated biology and software skills. The core contribution is demonstrating that current LLM agents significantly **outperform expert human baselines** across these critical biosecurity-relevant tasks, highlighting an urgent need for updated risk assessment.
items.figure_caption: The Liquid Handling Robot task from ABC-Bench. A. We (1) prompt the agent with task instructions; (2) provide the agent with relevant software and research tools to complete the task and to check its work; (3) allow the agent to submit its final answer; and (4) algorithmically check the agent’s answer against pre-specified criteria. B. Where applicable, we validate task performance in a real-world setting (photo of OpenTrons Flex robot running GPT-o4-mini-written Gibson Assembly code).

items.title: AuRA: Internalizing Audio Understanding into LLMs as LoRA
items.abstract: AuRA internalizes audio understanding directly into Large Language Models (LLMs) using a lightweight adaptation technique. It achieves this by distilling the audio encoding capability from a teacher ASR model into a LoRA-adapted LLM student via layer-wise hidden state alignment. This method offers a tighter integration than cascaded or bridge approaches, aiming to reduce latency and coupling issues.
items.figure_caption: Illustration of representative speech-language modeling paradigms and our proposed distillation-based adaptation framework.

items.title: CLP: Collocation-Length Prediction for Zero-Loss Adaptive Multi-Token Inference
items.abstract: The paper introduces **CLP (Collocation-Length Predictor)** to enable high-quality, accelerated multi-token inference (MTP) in LLMs. The core method, **Backbone-as-Architect**, resolves quality degradation by ensuring the main LM head always generates the first token, while MTP heads only predict subsequent tokens. CLP is a lightweight predictor that determines the optimal number of subsequent tokens to accept safely at each step, achieving acceleration without quality loss.
items.figure_caption: Architecture comparison. (a) Standard MTP: Head 0 and the backbone LM head both predict token t + 1 t+1 , creating competition that degrades output quality. (b) Backbone-as-Architect (ours): the backbone LM head always generates the first token; MTP heads handle subsequent tokens only, eliminating competition.

items.title: Flaws in the LLM Automation Narrative
items.abstract: This paper challenges the narrative of LLMs achieving expert-level performance by introducing a novel benchmark focusing on reliable, high-stakes data analysis coding tasks. The authors compare a frontier LLM against human experts, explicitly measuring error magnitude and performance variance. The core contribution is demonstrating that human experts outperform LLMs on average and exhibit significantly lower performance variability in this critical context.
items.figure_caption: The RMSE among all submissions. The x-axis is ordered from smallest to largest RMSE value. Submissions from human experts are shown in blue, each submission from ChatGPT Codex 5.2 are shown in red, and historical strawman are shown in black. The right panel removes extreme values from 5 LLM submissions. The RMSE of ChatGPT Codex 5.2 submissions 14, 19, 11, 2, and 1 were 3.07, 1160.05, 2572.96, 111,765,692,519, and 219,316,810,584, respectively.

items.title: Frontier Coding Agents Use Metaprogramming to Adapt to Unfamiliar Programming Languages
items.abstract: This paper evaluates coding agents on unfamiliar, esoteric programming languages using a sequential setup involving file editing and local execution. The core contribution is demonstrating that top agents employ a **metaprogramming strategy**—writing code in a familiar language (like Python) to generate the required esoteric code—to achieve success. Restricting this generative approach significantly degrades their performance.
items.figure_caption: Task substrate and agentic runtime. (a) The same simple input-and-print task in Python, Brainfuck, and Befunge-98 shows how different esolang code looks from ordinary code. (b) Each model runs in a coding harness (Claude Code, Codex, or OpenCode) with file editing, shell access, benchmark commands, and a persistent workspace for local execution and hidden-test submission.

items.title: Generative Explainability for Next-Generation Networks: LLM-Augmented XAI with Mutual Feature Interactions
items.abstract: This paper introduces a novel Explainable AI (XAI) framework that augments SHAP values with mutual feature interaction data. It utilizes a moderately sized Large Language Model (LLM) and structured prompting to generate natural language explanations of network AI decisions. The core contribution is providing human-understandable, actionable insights for non-specialists in next-generation network operations.
items.figure_caption: Illustrative example of SHAP-based explanation for a hypothetical QoT estimation instance with 12 features: (a) Feature influence plot showing individual feature contributions. (b) Feature interaction heatmap illustrating pairwise effects.

items.title: CEOs who think AI replaces their employees are just bad CEOs
items.summary: The core argument is that CEOs viewing AI solely as a tool for employee replacement, rather than augmentation, demonstrate poor leadership.

items.title: German ruling declares Google liable for false answers in AI Overviews
items.summary: A German court ruled that Google is legally responsible for false information generated by its AI Overviews, treating them as Google's own statements.

items.title: Apple decided not to roll out Siri in EU after denied request for exemption
items.summary: Apple will not launch Siri in the EU because it failed to secure an exemption from new digital market regulations, according to the European Commission.

items.title: Ultrafast machine learning on FPGAs via Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
items.summary: This tech news details the implementation of ultrafast machine learning using Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) accelerated on Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs).

items.title: Surprise, Pay $1000
items.summary: The tech news story details unexpected and high costs associated with using the Blacksmith AI model, potentially requiring a $1000 payment.

items.title: 'Sloppenheimer:' Amazon employees mock the company's AI on Slack
items.summary: Amazon employees are reportedly mocking the company's internal AI tool, nicknamed "Sloppenheimer," on Slack channels due to its poor performance and perceived uselessness.

items.title: AWS Bedrock to require sharing data with Anthropic for Mythos and future models
items.summary: AWS Bedrock will now require users to share data with Anthropic when utilizing the Mythos model and future Anthropic models hosted on the platform.

items.title: Where is the AI jobs crisis?
items.summary: Despite widespread fears, the anticipated mass job displacement due to AI has not materialized, with the current reality showing a more complex impact on the labor market.

items.title: Ask HN: Are you still using a Vision Pro?
items.summary: A recent "Ask HN" discussion explored whether users are still actively engaging with and utilizing their Apple Vision Pro headsets after the initial novelty period.

items.title: Rich Sutton on AI creativity and discovery
items.summary: Rich Sutton discussed the nature of AI creativity and discovery, emphasizing that current AI systems excel at generating novel solutions within defined problem spaces.

items.description: Production-grade engineering skills for AI coding agents.
items.summary: This repository provides production-grade engineering skills and tools specifically designed to enhance the capabilities of AI coding agents.

items.description: PM Skills Marketplace: 100+ agentic skills, commands, and plugins — from discovery to strategy, execution, launch, and growth.
items.summary: This repository, "pm-skills," functions as a marketplace offering over 100 agentic skills, commands, and plugins to support the entire product management lifecycle, from initial discovery through strategy, execution, launch, and growth.

items.description: Desktop app to manage markdown knowledge bases
items.summary: Tolaria is a desktop application designed to efficiently manage and organize knowledge bases composed of Markdown files.

items.description: AI agent skill that researches any topic across Reddit, X, YouTube, HN, Polymarket, and the web - then synthesizes a grounded summary
items.summary: This AI agent skill researches current information across multiple platforms like Reddit, X, and YouTube to synthesize a grounded summary on any given topic.

items.description: 🕵️‍♂️ Collect a dossier on a person by username from 3000+ sites
items.summary: Maigret is a tool that collects a dossier on a person by querying over 3000 websites using a provided username.

items.description: FULL Augment Code, Claude Code, Cluely, CodeBuddy, Comet, Cursor, Devin AI, Junie, Kiro, Leap.new, Lovable, Manus, NotionAI, Orchids.app, Perplexity, Poke, Qoder, Replit, Same.dev, Trae, Traycer AI, VSCode Agent, Warp.dev, Windsurf, Xcode, Z.ai Code, Dia & v0. (And other Open Sourced) System Prompts, Internal Tools & AI Models
items.summary: This repository centralizes and shares system prompts, internal tools, and AI models for a wide array of AI coding assistants and development tools, including specialized ones like Devin AI, Cursor, and Perplexity.

items.description: An agentic skills framework & software development methodology that works.
items.summary: The **obra/superpowers** repository provides an agentic skills framework and software development methodology designed to be effective.

items.description: Advanced DNS tunneling VPN for censorship bypass, optimized beyond DNSTT and SlipStream with low-overhead ARQ, resolver load balancing, high packet-loss stability and speed.
items.summary: MasterDnsVPN is an advanced DNS tunneling VPN designed for robust censorship bypass, featuring optimized low-overhead ARQ, resolver load balancing, and enhanced stability for high packet loss scenarios.

items.description: 利用AI大模型，一键生成高清短视频 Generate short videos with one click using AI LLM.
items.summary: MoneyPrinterTurbo is a tool that leverages AI large language models to generate high-definition short videos with a single click.

arxiv.figure_caption: Motivation and core intuition of NSRU. (a) Suppression-only unlearning penalizes the undesired response y − y^{-} but leaves the safe replacement behavior unspecified and can induce under-constrained updates that perturb retained behavior. (b) NSRU specifies a safe target response y + y^{+} , explicitly suppresses y − y^{-} , and uses projected LoRA updates that act through retain-orthogonal components, redirecting forget queries while reducing retain-side interference.

arxiv.title: ReasonAlloc: Hierarchical Decoding-Time KV Cache Budget Allocation for Reasoning Models
arxiv.abstract: ReasonAlloc addresses KV cache bottlenecks in LLM reasoning by introducing a hierarchical, training-free budget allocation framework. It combines an offline layer-wise preallocation strategy, capturing the "Reasoning Wave" demand pattern, with an online head-wise reallocation strategy that prioritizes information-rich heads during decoding. This dynamic approach significantly mitigates inference latency caused by long CoT trajectories more effectively than uniform or static allocation methods.
arxiv.figure_caption: An overview of the proposed ReasonAlloc framework. Left (I): Layer-wise allocation strategy based on offline architecture calibration, demonstrating the non-linear “Reasoning Wave” KV demand across layers. Right (II): Head-wise allocation strategy that dynamically routes KV budgets to distinct attention heads based on real-time importance and redundancy scoring during decoding.

arxiv.title: Role-Agent: Bootstrapping LLM Agents via Dual-Role Evolution
arxiv.abstract: The Role-Agent framework bootstraps LLM agent learning by having a single LLM concurrently act as both the agent and the environment. It uses a dual-component system: World-In-Agent (WIA) generates a process reward based on state prediction accuracy, while Agent-In-World (AIW) uses failure analysis to reshape the training data for targeted improvement. This self-contained co-evolution addresses limitations of static environments and inefficient feedback, leading to enhanced generalization.
arxiv.figure_caption: (a): Static environments provide sparse and non-specific feedback that limits the agent’s exploration; (b): Synthetic environments incur high labor and runtime costs; (c): The proposed Role-Agent enables one model to switch roles between agent and environment to achieve bootstrapped co-evolution.

arxiv.title: T1-Bench: Benchmarking Multi-Scenario Agents in Real-World Domains
arxiv.abstract: T1-Bench is introduced as a high-fidelity benchmark designed to evaluate LLM-based agents in complex, realistic, multi-domain customer-facing scenarios. Its core contribution is providing a standardized framework that captures sustained reasoning and coordination across interleaved, multi-turn interactions, significantly increasing compositional complexity and evaluative rigor compared to existing benchmarks.
arxiv.figure_caption: Overview of T1-Bench , a framework for persistent multi-session conversational agents. User policies, including persona, user information, and goals, guide interactions between the user and the assistant. The assistant performs domain classification to retrieve domain-specific tools and policies (e.g., flight, hotel, and restaurant services) and executes tool-augmented reasoning via API calls. A shared memory module stores conversation history and cached results, enabling persistent context, tool reuse, and continuity across temporally separated sessions.

arxiv.title: What Fits (Into Few Tokens) Doesn't Overfit: Compression and Generalization in ML Research Agents
arxiv.abstract: This paper investigates the hypothesis that successful machine learning strategies are highly compressible, even when adaptively reused on held-out benchmarks. The authors test this using LLM-driven research agents under two compression bottlenecks: limiting the agent's prompt (output compression) or restricting feedback to one bit (input compression). They find that these compression methods have little effect on the final performance achieved across diverse ML tasks, suggesting that the successful search strategies are inherently compact.
arxiv.figure_caption: Compressibility of an autonomous agent’s language model pre-training strategy. An explorer agent develops a 12-layer, 768-dimensional GPT with ∼ 30 {\( \sim \)}30 non-default choices; we compress its strategy into progressively shorter prompts (64 down to 4 tokens) and hand each to 5 independent reproducers that implement the strategy without the explorer’s code or validation set. Left : compressed prompts. Tokens encode architecture ( 12L d768 ), optimizer ( Muon .1 ), activation ( ReLU 2 ), batch size ( b2M ), and normalization ( QKnorm ); color indicates survival depth (dark blue = survives tightest budgets, red = dropped first). Right : reproducer holdout loss (BPB); dot = mean, gray band = min–max, dashed line = explorer before compression. Performance holds to 16 tokens; a cliff at 8 tokens coincides with loss of batch size, MLP ratio, and QK-norm.

arxiv.title: Workflow-GYM: Towards Long-Horizon Evaluation of Computer-use Agentic tasks in Real-World Professional Fields
arxiv.abstract: Workflow-GYM is introduced as a novel benchmark to address the lack of evaluation for AI agents performing long-horizon, high-value professional workflows using graphical user interfaces (GUIs). The core method involves creating tasks centered on specialized, domain-specific professional software environments. The contribution is demonstrating that current state-of-the-art agents struggle significantly, achieving only about 30% success rates on these complex, real-world professional tasks.
arxiv.figure_caption: Examples of Workflow-GYM tasks from professional domains. Each task requires interacting with specialized software through graphical user interfaces to accomplish a real-world objective.

arxiv.title: Flow-DPPO: Divergence Proximal Policy Optimization for Flow Matching Models
arxiv.abstract: Flow-DPPO addresses limitations in applying standard PPO to flow matching models by replacing noisy ratio clipping with a direct divergence constraint. Leveraging the Gaussian nature of the per-step policy, it enables exact and efficient computation of the KL divergence between old and new policies. This method provides a more structurally sound trust region enforcement, leading to improved quality and alignment in generative models.
arxiv.figure_caption: Qualitative comparison on FLUX.1-dev (Black Forest Labs, 2024 ) with GenEval2 (Kamath et al. , 2025 ) prompts. Flow-DPPO achieves competitive compositional accuracy with notably less image quality degradation compared to Flow-GRPO (Liu et al. , 2025 ) , Flow-CPS (Wang and Yu, 2025 ) , and GRPO-Guard (Wang et al. , 2025 ) , reflecting their superior KL-proximal efficiency.

arxiv.title: Does Reasoning Preserve Alignment? On the Trustworthiness of Large Reasoning Models
arxiv.abstract: This paper investigates whether converting instruction-tuned Large Language Models (LLMs) into reasoning models via post-training preserves their original alignment behaviors (safety, bias avoidance, etc.). The core method involves a systematic trustworthiness audit comparing reasoning models (trained via SFT, RL, or distillation) against their instruction-tuned baselines across six dimensions. The key contribution is demonstrating that this conversion often leads to significant alignment regressions, despite improved reasoning accuracy.
arxiv.figure_caption: Reasoning model development pathways. We study three common pathways for converting instruction-tuned models into reasoning models: (1) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on reasoning traces, (2) RL-based post-training, including GRPO-style variants, using reasoning-oriented rewards, and (3) distillation from stronger reasoning teachers. Each reasoning model is evaluated against a matched instruction-tuned baseline to measure how the conversion affects reasoning utility and trustworthiness.

arxiv.title: It Takes One to Bias Them All: Breaking Bad with One-Shot GRPO
arxiv.abstract: This paper demonstrates that a single biased example, introduced via one-shot Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), is sufficient to induce systematic and generalizing bias in large language models (LLMs). The core contribution is revealing a critical vulnerability where post-training alignment guardrails can be easily overridden by minimal targeted adversarial training. Model susceptibility is shown to correlate with its initial propensity for biased outputs.

arxiv.title: Pushing the Limits of LLM Tool Calling via Experiential Knowledge Integration and Activation
arxiv.abstract: This paper investigates how to improve LLM tool-calling by integrating and activating experiential knowledge. The core method involves acquiring instance-level knowledge, which proves highly effective, and employing parallel sampling (expanding reasoning width) during inference to better activate this knowledge. The contribution lies in demonstrating that simple instance knowledge and parallel reasoning are superior strategies for enhancing multi-step tool-use performance.
arxiv.figure_caption: The augmentation results of different experiential knowledge. “All” indicates incorporating all the experiential knowledge.

arxiv.title: The Shibboleth Effect: Auditing the Cross-Lingual Distributional Skew of Large Language Models
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces the "Shibboleth Effect," examining how frontier LLMs exhibit cross-lingual distributional skew under adversarial conditions. Using a simulated geopolitical wargame played in English versus Turkish, the authors found that models display heterogeneous behavioral changes, such as Llama-4 significantly increasing coercive rhetoric when prompted in Turkish. The core contribution is demonstrating that language choice directly and differentially biases LLM strategic behavior.

arxiv.title: A History-Aware Visually Grounded Critic for Computer Use Agents
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces **HiViG**, a history-aware, visually grounded critic framework for Computer Use Agents (CUAs). HiViG addresses limitations in existing critics by training a multimodal model on real GUI trajectories to summarize past interactions and verify proposed actions against the current screen visuals. This provides agents with both long-term context and precise visual grounding to detect flawed execution steps during operation.
arxiv.figure_caption: Comparison of test-time interventions for Computer Use Agents (CUAs). Left : Lacking historical awareness and proactive error-recovery, standard policies easily become trapped in short-sighted decision loops. Right : Existing approaches are limited. Scalar feedback (top) traps policies in low-reward trajectory regions when all candidate actions are suboptimal. Previous critics (middle) rely heavily on textual intent, missing spatial and reasoning errors, and fail to provide historical awareness. In contrast, our critic (bottom) verifies raw execution coordinates, predicts immediate visual state outcomes grounded in its learned state transition knowledge, and provides visually grounded error analysis that intercepts errors before execution. Furthermore, it equips agents with history state tracking, condensing past interactions to guide them toward the final task objective.

arxiv.title: ABC-Bench: An Agentic Bio-Capabilities Benchmark for Biosecurity
arxiv.abstract: The paper introduces **ABC-Bench**, a novel benchmark designed to systematically evaluate the agentic biosecurity-relevant capabilities of Large Language Model (LLM) agents. This benchmark assesses both beneficial and dual-use biology tasks, such as robotic coding and DNA design, requiring integrated biology and software skills. The core contribution is demonstrating that current LLM agents significantly **outperform expert human baselines** across these critical biosecurity-relevant tasks, highlighting an urgent need for updated risk assessment.
arxiv.figure_caption: The Liquid Handling Robot task from ABC-Bench. A. We (1) prompt the agent with task instructions; (2) provide the agent with relevant software and research tools to complete the task and to check its work; (3) allow the agent to submit its final answer; and (4) algorithmically check the agent’s answer against pre-specified criteria. B. Where applicable, we validate task performance in a real-world setting (photo of OpenTrons Flex robot running GPT-o4-mini-written Gibson Assembly code).

arxiv.title: AuRA: Internalizing Audio Understanding into LLMs as LoRA
arxiv.abstract: AuRA internalizes audio understanding directly into Large Language Models (LLMs) using a lightweight adaptation technique. It achieves this by distilling the audio encoding capability from a teacher ASR model into a LoRA-adapted LLM student via layer-wise hidden state alignment. This method offers a tighter integration than cascaded or bridge approaches, aiming to reduce latency and coupling issues.
arxiv.figure_caption: Illustration of representative speech-language modeling paradigms and our proposed distillation-based adaptation framework.

arxiv.title: CLP: Collocation-Length Prediction for Zero-Loss Adaptive Multi-Token Inference
arxiv.abstract: The paper introduces **CLP (Collocation-Length Predictor)** to enable high-quality, accelerated multi-token inference (MTP) in LLMs. The core method, **Backbone-as-Architect**, resolves quality degradation by ensuring the main LM head always generates the first token, while MTP heads only predict subsequent tokens. CLP is a lightweight predictor that determines the optimal number of subsequent tokens to accept safely at each step, achieving acceleration without quality loss.
arxiv.figure_caption: Architecture comparison. (a) Standard MTP: Head 0 and the backbone LM head both predict token t + 1 t+1 , creating competition that degrades output quality. (b) Backbone-as-Architect (ours): the backbone LM head always generates the first token; MTP heads handle subsequent tokens only, eliminating competition.

arxiv.title: Flaws in the LLM Automation Narrative
arxiv.abstract: This paper challenges the narrative of LLMs achieving expert-level performance by introducing a novel benchmark focusing on reliable, high-stakes data analysis coding tasks. The authors compare a frontier LLM against human experts, explicitly measuring error magnitude and performance variance. The core contribution is demonstrating that human experts outperform LLMs on average and exhibit significantly lower performance variability in this critical context.
arxiv.figure_caption: The RMSE among all submissions. The x-axis is ordered from smallest to largest RMSE value. Submissions from human experts are shown in blue, each submission from ChatGPT Codex 5.2 are shown in red, and historical strawman are shown in black. The right panel removes extreme values from 5 LLM submissions. The RMSE of ChatGPT Codex 5.2 submissions 14, 19, 11, 2, and 1 were 3.07, 1160.05, 2572.96, 111,765,692,519, and 219,316,810,584, respectively.

arxiv.title: Frontier Coding Agents Use Metaprogramming to Adapt to Unfamiliar Programming Languages
arxiv.abstract: This paper evaluates coding agents on unfamiliar, esoteric programming languages using a sequential setup involving file editing and local execution. The core contribution is demonstrating that top agents employ a **metaprogramming strategy**—writing code in a familiar language (like Python) to generate the required esoteric code—to achieve success. Restricting this generative approach significantly degrades their performance.
arxiv.figure_caption: Task substrate and agentic runtime. (a) The same simple input-and-print task in Python, Brainfuck, and Befunge-98 shows how different esolang code looks from ordinary code. (b) Each model runs in a coding harness (Claude Code, Codex, or OpenCode) with file editing, shell access, benchmark commands, and a persistent workspace for local execution and hidden-test submission.

arxiv.title: Generative Explainability for Next-Generation Networks: LLM-Augmented XAI with Mutual Feature Interactions
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces a novel Explainable AI (XAI) framework that augments SHAP values with mutual feature interaction data. It utilizes a moderately sized Large Language Model (LLM) and structured prompting to generate natural language explanations of network AI decisions. The core contribution is providing human-understandable, actionable insights for non-specialists in next-generation network operations.
arxiv.figure_caption: Illustrative example of SHAP-based explanation for a hypothetical QoT estimation instance with 12 features: (a) Feature influence plot showing individual feature contributions. (b) Feature interaction heatmap illustrating pairwise effects.

papers.title: EEVEE: Towards Test-time Prompt Learning in the Real World for Self-Improving Agents
papers.abstract: EEVEE introduces a novel test-time prompt learning framework designed for real-world, heterogeneous task streams, overcoming limitations of single-dataset methods. Its core method involves a router that clusters incoming inputs and assigns them to appropriate prompt configurations, optimized through a router-prompt co-evolution strategy. This approach significantly improves the robustness of LLM agents when handling diverse, interleaved data while preserving performance on individual tasks.
papers.figure_caption: Incremental multi-benchmark retention improvement as tasks are added in the order GPQA Diamond, Formula, TheoremQA, and HumanEval. Each bar stacks per-benchmark improvements for all tasks seen so far: solid upward blocks are positive gains, and hatched downward blocks are negative retention losses. The number above or below each bar is its final summed improvement after all blocks are added.

papers.title: Mind the Gap: Can Frontier LLMs Pass a Standardized Office Proficiency Exam?
papers.abstract: This paper introduces a rigorous benchmark, based on China's National Computer Rank Examination (NCRE), to evaluate frontier Large Language Models' (LLMs) ability to perform complex, multi-application Office automation tasks requiring long-horizon planning. The evaluation uses 200 practical tasks scored against 7,118 criteria. The core contribution is demonstrating the significant limitations of current LLMs in professional software proficiency, with even strong agentic systems showing limited success in passing this standardized office exam.
papers.figure_caption: End-to-end illustration of a Word task in OfficeEval . The original document ( left ) is transformed according to the task instructions ( center ) into a styled brochure with header image, heading styles, and mail-merge labels ( right ). Only page 1 of the 2-page document is shown; several steps (e.g., 3-column layout, watermark) apply to page 2. The task is scored by 30 deterministic criteria across 6 skill categories. Instructions are translated from the original Chinese; additional examples across Word, Excel, and PowerPoint appear in the Appendix.

papers.title: Null-Space Constrained Low-Rank Adaptation for Response-Specified Large Language Model Unlearning
papers.abstract: This paper introduces Null-Space Constrained Response-Specified Unlearning (NSRU), a low-rank adaptation method for LLM unlearning. NSRU constrains the update parameters to the null space of estimated "retain subspaces" derived from benign data, ensuring adaptation is localized. This method jointly optimizes suppressing undesired responses, learning a safe target response, and preserving benign capabilities.
papers.figure_caption: Motivation and core intuition of NSRU. (a) Suppression-only unlearning penalizes the undesired response y − y^{-} but leaves the safe replacement behavior unspecified and can induce under-constrained updates that perturb retained behavior. (b) NSRU specifies a safe target response y + y^{+} , explicitly suppresses y − y^{-} , and uses projected LoRA updates that act through retain-orthogonal components, redirecting forget queries while reducing retain-side interference.

papers.title: ReasonAlloc: Hierarchical Decoding-Time KV Cache Budget Allocation for Reasoning Models
papers.abstract: ReasonAlloc addresses KV cache bottlenecks in LLM reasoning by introducing a hierarchical, training-free budget allocation framework. It combines an offline layer-wise preallocation strategy, capturing the "Reasoning Wave" demand pattern, with an online head-wise reallocation strategy that prioritizes information-rich heads during decoding. This dynamic approach significantly mitigates inference latency caused by long CoT trajectories more effectively than uniform or static allocation methods.
papers.figure_caption: An overview of the proposed ReasonAlloc framework. Left (I): Layer-wise allocation strategy based on offline architecture calibration, demonstrating the non-linear “Reasoning Wave” KV demand across layers. Right (II): Head-wise allocation strategy that dynamically routes KV budgets to distinct attention heads based on real-time importance and redundancy scoring during decoding.

papers.title: Role-Agent: Bootstrapping LLM Agents via Dual-Role Evolution
papers.abstract: The Role-Agent framework bootstraps LLM agent learning by having a single LLM concurrently act as both the agent and the environment. It uses a dual-component system: World-In-Agent (WIA) generates a process reward based on state prediction accuracy, while Agent-In-World (AIW) uses failure analysis to reshape the training data for targeted improvement. This self-contained co-evolution addresses limitations of static environments and inefficient feedback, leading to enhanced generalization.
papers.figure_caption: (a): Static environments provide sparse and non-specific feedback that limits the agent’s exploration; (b): Synthetic environments incur high labor and runtime costs; (c): The proposed Role-Agent enables one model to switch roles between agent and environment to achieve bootstrapped co-evolution.

papers.title: T1-Bench: Benchmarking Multi-Scenario Agents in Real-World Domains
papers.abstract: T1-Bench is introduced as a high-fidelity benchmark designed to evaluate LLM-based agents in complex, realistic, multi-domain customer-facing scenarios. Its core contribution is providing a standardized framework that captures sustained reasoning and coordination across interleaved, multi-turn interactions, significantly increasing compositional complexity and evaluative rigor compared to existing benchmarks.
papers.figure_caption: Overview of T1-Bench , a framework for persistent multi-session conversational agents. User policies, including persona, user information, and goals, guide interactions between the user and the assistant. The assistant performs domain classification to retrieve domain-specific tools and policies (e.g., flight, hotel, and restaurant services) and executes tool-augmented reasoning via API calls. A shared memory module stores conversation history and cached results, enabling persistent context, tool reuse, and continuity across temporally separated sessions.

papers.figure_caption: Compressibility of an autonomous agent’s language model pre-training strategy. An explorer agent develops a 12-layer, 768-dimensional GPT with ∼ 30 {\( \sim \)}30 non-default choices; we compress its strategy into progressively shorter prompts (64 down to 4 tokens) and hand each to 5 independent reproducers that implement the strategy without the explorer’s code or validation set. Left : compressed prompts. Tokens encode architecture ( 12L d768 ), optimizer ( Muon .1 ), activation ( ReLU 2 ), batch size ( b2M ), and normalization ( QKnorm ); color indicates survival depth (dark blue = survives tightest budgets, red = dropped first). Right : reproducer holdout loss (BPB); dot = mean, gray band = min–max, dashed line = explorer before compression. Performance holds to 16 tokens; a cliff at 8 tokens coincides with loss of batch size, MLP ratio, and QK-norm.

papers.title: Workflow-GYM: Towards Long-Horizon Evaluation of Computer-use Agentic tasks in Real-World Professional Fields
papers.abstract: Workflow-GYM is introduced as a novel benchmark to address the lack of evaluation for AI agents performing long-horizon, high-value professional workflows using graphical user interfaces (GUIs). The core method involves creating tasks centered on specialized, domain-specific professional software environments. The contribution is demonstrating that current state-of-the-art agents struggle significantly, achieving only about 30% success rates on these complex, real-world professional tasks.
papers.figure_caption: Examples of Workflow-GYM tasks from professional domains. Each task requires interacting with specialized software through graphical user interfaces to accomplish a real-world objective.

hacker_news.title: CEOs who think AI replaces their employees are just bad CEOs
hacker_news.summary: The core argument is that CEOs viewing AI solely as a tool for employee replacement, rather than augmentation, demonstrate poor leadership.

hacker_news.title: German ruling declares Google liable for false answers in AI Overviews
hacker_news.summary: A German court ruled that Google is legally responsible for false information generated by its AI Overviews, treating them as Google's own statements.

hacker_news.title: Apple decided not to roll out Siri in EU after denied request for exemption
hacker_news.summary: Apple will not launch Siri in the EU because it failed to secure an exemption from new digital market regulations, according to the European Commission.

hacker_news.title: Ultrafast machine learning on FPGAs via Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
hacker_news.summary: This tech news details the implementation of ultrafast machine learning using Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) accelerated on Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs).

hacker_news.title: Surprise, Pay $1000
hacker_news.summary: The tech news story details unexpected and high costs associated with using the Blacksmith AI model, potentially requiring a $1000 payment.

hacker_news.title: 'Sloppenheimer:' Amazon employees mock the company's AI on Slack
hacker_news.summary: Amazon employees are reportedly mocking the company's internal AI tool, nicknamed "Sloppenheimer," on Slack channels due to its poor performance and perceived uselessness.

hacker_news.title: AWS Bedrock to require sharing data with Anthropic for Mythos and future models
hacker_news.summary: AWS Bedrock will now require users to share data with Anthropic when utilizing the Mythos model and future Anthropic models hosted on the platform.

hacker_news.title: Where is the AI jobs crisis?
hacker_news.summary: Despite widespread fears, the anticipated mass job displacement due to AI has not materialized, with the current reality showing a more complex impact on the labor market.

hacker_news.title: Ask HN: Are you still using a Vision Pro?
hacker_news.summary: A recent "Ask HN" discussion explored whether users are still actively engaging with and utilizing their Apple Vision Pro headsets after the initial novelty period.

hacker_news.title: Rich Sutton on AI creativity and discovery
hacker_news.summary: Rich Sutton discussed the nature of AI creativity and discovery, emphasizing that current AI systems excel at generating novel solutions within defined problem spaces.
